name: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines
excerpt: Resistance from native Caribs prevented colonization on Saint Vincent until 1719. France and England disputed the island for most of the 18th century, but it was ceded to England in 1783.

quick_facts.government: parliamentary democracy under a constitutional monarchy; a Commonwealth realm
quick_facts.languages: English, Vincentian Creole English, French patois
quick_facts.religions: Protestant 75% (Pentecostal 27.6%, Anglican 13.9%, Seventh Day Adventist 11.6%, Baptist 8.9%, Methodist 8.7%, Evangelical 3.8%, Salvation Army 0.3%, Presbyterian/Congregational 0.3%), Roman Catholic 6.3%, Rastafarian 1.1%, Jehovah's Witness 0.8%, other 4.7%, none 7.5%, unspecified 4.7% (2012 est.)
quick_facts.ethnic_groups: African descent 71.2%, mixed 23%, Indigenous 3%, East Indian/Indian 1.1%, European 1.5%, other 0.2% (2012 est.)
quick_facts.climate: tropical; little seasonal temperature variation; rainy season (May to November)
quick_facts.major_exports: ships, wheat flours, animal food, shellfish, construction vehicles (2023)

summaries.geography_summary: the administration of the islands of the Grenadines group is divided between Saint Vincent and the Grenadines and Grenada; Saint Vincent and the Grenadines is composed of 32 islands and cays
summaries.history_summary: Resistance from native Caribs prevented colonization on Saint Vincent until 1719. France and England disputed the island for most of the 18th century, but it was ceded to England in 1783. The British prized Saint Vincent because of its fertile soil, which allowed for thriving slave-run plantations of sugar, coffee, indigo, tobacco, cotton, and cocoa. In 1834, the British abolished slavery. Immigration of indentured servants eased the ensuing labor shortage, as did subsequent immigrant waves from Portugal and East India. Conditions remained harsh for both former slaves and immigrant agricultural workers, however, as depressed world sugar prices kept the economy stagnant until the early 1900s. The economy then went into a period of decline, with many landowners abandoning their estates and leaving the land to be cultivated by liberated slaves. Between 1960 and 1962, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines was a separate administrative unit of the Federation of the West Indies. Autonomy was granted in 1969 and independence in 1979. In 2021, the eruption of the La Soufrière volcano in the north of Saint Vincent destroyed much of Saint Vincent’s most productive agricultural lands. Unlike most of its tourism-dependent neighbors, the Vincentian economy is primarily agricultural.
summaries.government_summary: parliamentary democracy under a constitutional monarchy; a Commonwealth realm. Independence: 27 October 1979 (from the UK)
summaries.economy_summary: upper middle-income Caribbean island economy; key agriculture and tourism sectors; environmentally fragile; diversifying economy across services, science and knowledge, and creative industries; CARICOM member and US Caribbean Basin Initiative beneficiary

suggested_reading: CIA World Factbook data (archived via factbook.json) | https://github.com/factbook/factbook.json

prayer_and_mission.prayer_intro: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines has a strong Protestant majority, including an existing Baptist witness, real ground to build on. Pray for these churches to pursue genuine biblical depth.
prayer_points: Pray for Saint Vincent and the Grenadines' extensive Baptist and Protestant churches to be strengthened in sound biblical doctrine.
Pray for pastors to be equipped in expository, Scripture-centered preaching.
Pray for genuine gospel conversion across a culture where church attendance is common.
prayer_and_mission.mission_emphasis: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines' strong Protestant majority is real ground to build on. Ongoing needs include deepening biblical literacy and expository preaching among existing churches.
